header.text: General Services Administration (GSA) RFQ1809121 TTS NEXT BPAs Support Area 5 Technical Capability

content.text: AIS delivers AI governance through a four-phase lifecycle framework — Intake → Build → Monitor → Audit — that establishes controls before any model enters production and sustains oversight through retirement.

Intake begins every engagement with a governance assessment: classifying AI systems by risk level, identifying human oversight requirements, defining approval workflows, and documenting data provenance. This classification directly maps to OMB M-25-21's high-impact AI designation criteria and ensures Chief AI Officer visibility from day one.

AIS architects AI systems for traceable, defensible outputs. For RAG — the dominant pattern in federal AI deployments — AIS implements source-cited retrieval so every AI-generated response links directly to the documents that informed it.

AIS implements automated bias scoring with reason-code explanations, using architecturally separated evaluation models to eliminate self-grading risk. Evaluation pipelines assess outputs against demographic and protected characteristics.

AIS deploys comprehensive AI monitoring using Azure Sentinel, Microsoft Defender, and custom observability dashboards tailored to AI-specific metrics including model drift detection, accuracy degradation tracking, and usage pattern analysis.

sections.heading: Evaluating When AI is the Appropriate Solution

content.text: AIS applies a structured decision framework to determine when AI adds genuine value versus when traditional software engineering, rules engines, or human processes are more effective, reliable, and cost-efficient.

The framework evaluates task complexity, data maturity, explainability requirements, and regulatory constraints before recommending an AI approach. Not every problem needs AI — and recommending against AI when appropriate builds more trust than defaulting to it.

sections.heading: Data Quality, Privacy, and Regulatory Alignment

content.text: AIS ensures that AI systems are built on a foundation of high-quality data, strong privacy protections, and full compliance with federal AI guidance.
AIS implements automated data profiling, validation pipelines, and lineage tracking to ensure training and inference data meets quality thresholds before any model consumes it.

AIS deploys privacy-preserving architectures including data minimization, tokenization, differential privacy where appropriate, and strict access controls aligned with FISMA and agency-specific privacy requirements.

AIS maintains active compliance with OMB M-25-21 (governance and Chief AI Officer requirements), M-25-22 (AI acquisition and vendor lock-in avoidance), M-26-04 (unbiased AI and bias testing), and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework across all engagements.

comments.author: Funk, Joe
comments.text: Instruction: Describe your approach to implementing Artificial Intelligence / Machine Learning (AI/ML) capabilities within a Federal environment, including how you address model governance, explainability, bias detection, and ongoing performance monitoring.
